module: Module 2: In Search of a Universal Theory
content: So now we look at the final argument of Weber, which is looking at, well, okay, this kind of spirit, this ideology led to flourishing of capitalism, which then spread everywhere and now what is the consequence for modern society? So he talks about how whenever you have the strict combination of conduct is very much regulated and you have strict limits of consumption, you'll see wealth accumulation. And then once you have this richest accumulating over generations, the discipline and frugality, that original sort of that fervor and passion and zeal that breaks down. And so you see this secularizing influence of wealth. So as capitalism slowly took over from these small pockets where you had these preachers and very religious folks, where it developed then grew into this entire world order, and it determines the lives of individuals who we are born into the system and we are pulled into this idea of continuous acquisition, continuous more and more and more work and money, like an irresistible force over which we don't have as much control as we think we do. And so the famous quote from Weber, 'The care for external goods should lie on the shoulders of a saint, like a light cloak, which can be thrown aside at any moment but fate decreed that the cloak should become an iron cage'. And so this care of external goods, which initially the spirit was that, you know, you don't really care about the external goods, you care about this, you know, this passionate zeal, right? So the care for external goods is like a light cloak, which lies on your shoulders, which you can throw aside at any moment. But fate has decreed that this cloak should now become an iron cage. And so you are like a rat in this cage, running, running, running more and more and more. So, 'Material goods have gained an increasing and finally an inexorable power over the lives of humans, as at no previous period in history'. So this is Weber speaking in the 1900s. And then Weber talks about how, 'Victorious capitalism no longer needs the support of this ethic. This religious thing is no longer needed. So the idea of duty in one's calling prowls about in our lives, like the ghost of dead religious beliefs'. But this idea of calling and vocation and duty, it still is there in our lives. It still prowls about in our lives, like the ghost of dead religious beliefs, that's the quote from Weber. And so in the field of the highest development of capitalism, the United States, the pursuit of wealth, stripped of its religious and ethical meaning tends to become associated with purely mundane passions, which often actually give it the character of a sport. So now I'll ask you to go back to that clip we watched from the Wall Street of Garden Gecko. And, you know, that whole movie, you get the sense that, you know, this moneymaking, it's like a spoke there's no real passion behind you are doing it as if it's a game. And if some people get hurt, some people don't. That's all par for the course. And that is exactly what Weber is saying, that all of those ideals are stripped away and all that is left is something which takes on a character of a sport. And so now we become, and a quote from Weber, 'Specialists Without Spirit, sensualists without heart, this nullity imagines that it has attained a level of civilization never before achieved'. So we are luxuriating in all these material goods and so we essential is without heart, and we imagine that we have reached some high level of civilization but it's actually a nullity. It's a void because there's no zeal behind it, that zeal the ethics is now gone. And so this is the basis of Weber's ideas about rationalization bureaucratization, where increasingly you do things based on quantifiable ideas about efficiency growth and where the zeal is to make things more and more rational and depersonalize them. In that process, we have lost the essence of human empathy and there is this disenchantment that we see when we look around in society. Of course, Weber gives, he is a, at the end of the day, he is a scholar and he is not an ideologist, so he is not saying that this religion is better or this spirit is better, or capitalism is better or something else is better. He is saying that we just, he wants to understand things. He wants to understand social change. He's not pushing and saying, this is better than this. So in the spirit of being a scholar, he says that we should continue studying how these belief systems ideologies, whether religious or secular, how do these belief systems shape institutions over time? And he says that we should perhaps avoid both purely economic materialistic explanations, which is what Marx is doing, or purely ideological belief system based explanations, because real history probably involves both. So this is the caveat that Weber ends with. Now so what is a key takeaway from Weber? The ideas behind the capitalism. What are those ideologies? Duty calling, asceticism, frugality, investment, continuous improvement in the surge of continuous growth. But then once capitalism took root it outgrew all of these ethics and has taken on the character of a sport, it has become an iron cage centralist without spirit. And now I would like to end this by giving you a couple of exercises where I want you to think about how all this relates to us. So I want you to look at your own society in which you are based and look at your own history, the history of your society, and I want you to look at it from both Marxist's perspective and Weber's perspective. So look at it from Marx's historical materialism perspective, where you look at the economic instruments, the relations of production, the modes of production and how that impacted the views and the philosophies. But also look at it from the other way around how views and philosophies are traditionalist or are supporting capitalism. I also want you to do a thought exercise to look at your own society again, and to see, can you identify the spirit of your society, just like Weber did. He identified the spirit of capitalism because that was the dominant system. And even to today, capitalism is the dominant system. But I want you to be more specific in your own specific district society that you are based in. What is the spirit? Is it exactly the same spirit as what Weber says, or is it more complex. What is that spirit? I want you to identify it. And with that, we lend this module on Marx and Weber.
